prompt: Edo-period ukiyo-e woodblock print, 1820s–1830s actor portrait. Depict overfitting as a figure whose garments and facial features are rendered with obsessive, meticulous detail — every fabric fold shown as geometric line patterns, every facial feature expressively detailed. The background is intentionally *simplified* — minimal tree rendered as flat indigo/dark blocks, almost abstract, vastly less detail than the figure. Stark asymmetrical diagonal composition; the figure dominates foreground, background recedes. Limited color palette: indigo, rust orange, cream, black, tan only — muted natural dye colors. All forms defined by decisive black outlines and flat, unmodulated color. NO gradients, NO photorealistic shading, NO soft edges. Visible woodblock texture and wood grain throughout. High contrast between figure and ground. Flat perspective — not Western vanishing point. The *tension between obsessive detail in the figure and radical simplification in the background* is the visual expression of overfitting. Reference the intricate precision of Hokusai's actor portraits and Hiroshige's figures, but deliberately simplify the surrounding environment. Pure Edo ukiyo-e visual grammar, authentically executed.
tradition_approach: Diagonal asymmetrical composition with foreground figure dominance. Obsessive geometric pattern work and linear detail in primary figure (fabric patterns, facial expression, line variation). Deliberately simplified, nearly abstract background (flat indigo/rust blocks, minimal detail). Muted natural-dye palette: indigo, rust, cream, black. Visible woodblock texture. Flat perspective, high figure-ground contrast. The detail-versus-simplification structural contrast IS the concept: visual embodiment of overfitting through compositional grammar, not metaphorical decoration.
